requirements: The core contributions of FlexiCache have been reproduced.

sub_tasks.requirements: Environment and infrastructure dependencies have been set up correctly.
FlexiCache is built atop vLLM, with the appropriate Python (3.12), PyTorch (2.6.0+cu124), Triton (3.2.0), Transformers (4.50.0), and CUDA (12.8 compatible) environment.
The hardware testbed used is an NVIDIA H100 GPU (94 GB HBM) with at least 256 GB of host DRAM and PCIe Gen5 (or equivalent CPU-GPU interconnect) available for KV transfer.
180 GB of pinned host memory is allocated for the offloaded KV cache pool.
The models and datasets have been acquired for evaluation.
meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct is available for inference.
mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 is available for inference.
mistralai/Mistral-Small-24B-Instruct-2501 is available for inference.
Qwen/Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct is available for inference.
meta-llama/Llama-3-8B-Instruct is available for inference (used for LServe comparison).
The LongBench dataset (zai-org/LongBench) is downloaded and available, covering all 16 tasks used for evaluation.
The L-Eval dataset is downloaded and available, covering tasks LongFQA, GovReport, CUAD, QMSum, Multi-News, Openreview, BigPatent, SPACE, SummScreen.
The offline KV-head stability profiling procedure has been implemented as described in Section 2.2.
Code records, for each sample, a tensor of shape [D, L, H, K] of per-decode-step top-K page indices for every layer and KV head.
The random-corrected overlap RCO(s, t) = (|S(s) ∩ S(t)|/K − K/N_t) / (1 − K/N_t) is computed between top-K page sets at decode step s and step t = s + Δ for 1 ≤ Δ < W.
The stability window size W is set equal to the stable-head reranking interval (16 in main experiments).
A per-head temporal stability score is computed by aggregating (averaging) RCO values over all pairs within each window and across windows and samples.
KV heads are classified as unstable (least stable 25%) and stable (remaining 75%) based on their aggregated stability scores.

Profiling produces a single per-model stable/unstable head partition that is reused across all downstream tasks at inference time (using the GovReport profile for LongBench tasks and an L-Eval Openreview profile for GovReport).
The KV-page importance scoring mechanism has been implemented as described in Section 3.2.
For each KV page, per-dimension minimum and maximum key vectors are maintained.
Page importance is computed as score(p) = sum over dimensions d of max(q_d * k_min_d, q_d * k_max_d), upper-bounding the maximum possible dot product between q and any key in page p.
Unstable heads are scored at every decode step, whereas stable heads are scored periodically (every 16 steps).
Between reranking steps, stable heads reuse the most recently computed top-K page set.
Scoring is skipped entirely for any layer composed solely of stable heads on non-rerank steps.
A dedicated MinMax cache stores min/max vectors on the GPU even when corresponding KV pages are offloaded to host memory.
The MinMax cache uses its own block manager allocating large blocks containing min/max vectors for up to 128 KV cache blocks each.
Sparse decode attention over top-K pages has been implemented (Goal G1).
The Triton-based Flash-Decoding kernel from vLLM has been modified to attend only to the top-K KV pages selected per head during decoding.
The sparse decode kernel supports per-head independent top-K page selection (each head can attend to a different subset of pages).
A page size of 16 tokens is used, with default top-K page budgets of 64 (1024 tokens) and 128 (2048 tokens).
Hierarchical KV-cache placement and management have been implemented as described in Section 3.3 (Goal G2).
For stable heads, only the top-K KV pages reside in GPU memory while the remaining pages are stored in host memory.
For unstable heads, the full KV cache for all pages resides in GPU memory and is never offloaded.
Host memory holds the full KV cache for stable heads; each KV page of a stable head is offloaded GPU→host exactly once.
After the prefill phase, a large initial GPU→host offload of stable-head KV cache occurs on a low-priority background CUDA stream while decoding continues on the main stream.
Incremental GPU→host offloads occur as new KV pages become full during decoding, overlapped with computation.
After periodic reranking, only the newly promoted top-K pages (in current top-K but not previous) are fetched host→GPU.
The scheduler pauses only the request being reloaded during host→GPU transfers while other requests in the batch continue decoding.
GPU blocks for pages no longer in the top-K are released by the block manager after their content has been offloaded.
Custom CUDA KV-transfer kernels have been implemented to reduce fragmentation overhead.
A custom CUDA kernel directly reads from and writes to pinned host KV cache via Unified Virtual Addressing (UVA), eliminating CPU-side gather/scatter.
KV-transfer kernels are launched on a low-priority background CUDA stream.
The grid size of transfer kernels is capped to keep SM occupancy low and reduce contention with compute kernels.
Large transfers are split into multiple smaller chunks to give the CUDA scheduler opportunities to interleave compute kernels.
Efficient block-table management has been implemented as described in Section 3.4.
The block table tensor is extended from shape (B, N) to (B, L, H, N) so that each (layer, head) pair has its own block mapping.
Independent block allocation, deallocation, and mapping is performed per head-layer pair.
Dirty tracking maintains a record of modified CPU-side block-table regions since the last GPU sync, and only those dirty segments are transferred to the GPU via a custom CUDA kernel.

During periodic stable-head reranking, a fused CUDA kernel (i) identifies evicted and promoted logical blocks, (ii) recycles physical blocks from evicted to promoted entries while updating the GPU-side block table in place, and (iii) emits a mapping from host KV blocks to GPU KV blocks for the subsequent host→GPU transfer.
A null block mechanism keeps the logical block table dense: deallocated entries are redirected to a null block excluded from attention.
FlexiCache controller components (Scheduler and Block Allocator) have been implemented.
The FlexiCache Scheduler pipelines compute and KV reloads across batched requests, pausing only requests undergoing reload.

The FlexiCache Block Allocator extends vLLM's allocator with hierarchical memory awareness, sizing GPU KV blocks for top-K stable pages plus all unstable pages, and assigning host KV blocks for the full KV cache of stable heads.
Baseline systems have been set up for comparison.
vLLM (with its Triton-based Flash-Decoding backend) is set up as a dense-attention baseline for system performance comparisons.
LServe (built on QServe) is set up as a sparse-attention baseline with token budget 384 for streaming heads and 4096 for retrieval heads.
For LServe accuracy comparison, its quantized-sparse configuration is compared against its quantized-only baseline (relative drop reported) rather than against an unquantized dense baseline.
Evaluation metrics and harnesses have been implemented.
For accuracy, the ratio of FlexiCache benchmark score over the dense-attention benchmark score for the same model is reported as the retention metric.
Total token throughput (prompt + output tokens per second) is measured for offline inference.
Mean Time Per Output Token (TPOT) and Time To First Token (TTFT) are measured for online serving.
Online serving uses a Poisson arrival process to inject requests at controlled request rates.
Section 2.2/4.2 cross-task overlap analyses of unstable heads have been reproduced.
Per-task unstable-head sets (each containing 25% of heads) are computed independently for each of the 8 evaluation tasks: Openreview, BigPatent, Multi-News, QMSum, GovReport, SPACE, CUAD, SummScreen.
The pairwise cross-task overlap matrix is computed as |A_i ∩ A_j| / |A_i| (with |A_i| = 64 for Llama-3.1-8B).
Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct's mean cross-task unstable-head overlap is approximately 0.83 (Table 1).
Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2's mean cross-task unstable-head overlap is approximately 0.81 (Table 2a).
Mistral-Small-24B-Instruct-2501's mean cross-task unstable-head overlap is approximately 0.76 (Table 2b).
Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct's mean cross-task unstable-head overlap is approximately 0.81 (Table 2c).
Figure 1's per-head mean RCO curves for layer 4 of Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on SPACE are reproduced, showing distinct stable (high RCO with slow decay) and unstable (consistently low RCO) heads.
Accuracy retention on LongBench (Section 4.3, Table 3) has been reproduced.

FlexiCache and dense baseline accuracy is measured on all 16 LongBench tasks (NarrativeQA, Qasper, MultiField-en, HotpotQA, 2WikiMQA, Musique, GovReport, QMSum, MultiNews, TREC, TriviaQA, SAMSum, PCount, PRe, Lcc, RB-P) using the official LongBench scoring scripts.
LongBench evaluation uses a token budget of 64 pages (1024 tokens), 64 unstable heads, and stable-head rerank frequency of 16.
The KV-head stability profile from L-Eval Openreview is used for the GovReport task in LongBench; the GovReport profile is used for all other LongBench tasks (no task-specific leakage).
Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct achieves an average FlexiCache/Dense accuracy ratio of approximately 1.00 across the 16 LongBench tasks (Table 3).
Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 achieves an average FlexiCache/Dense accuracy ratio of approximately 0.99 across the 16 LongBench tasks (Table 3).
Accuracy retention on L-Eval (Section 4.3, Table 5) has been reproduced.

FlexiCache is evaluated on all 9 L-Eval tasks (LongFQA, GovReport, CUAD, QMSum, Multi-News, Openreview, BigPatent, SPACE, SummScreen) using the same fixed KV-head stability profile from LongBench GovReport.
Ablation 'W/O Reranking' (top-K computed once and never updated) is implemented and evaluated.
Ablation 'W/O Unstable Heads' (all heads treated as stable) is implemented and evaluated.
FlexiCache is evaluated with token budgets of both 1024 and 2048 tokens.
Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct FlexiCache-2048 achieves an average L-Eval accuracy ratio of approximately 0.99 versus dense (Table 5).
Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 FlexiCache-2048 achieves an average L-Eval accuracy ratio of approximately 0.99 versus dense (Table 5).
Mistral-Small-24B-Instruct-2501 FlexiCache-2048 achieves an average L-Eval accuracy ratio of approximately 1.00 versus dense (Table 5).
Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct FlexiCache-2048 achieves an average L-Eval accuracy ratio of approximately 1.00 versus dense (Table 5).
Llama-3-8B-Instruct LServe achieves an average L-Eval accuracy ratio of approximately 0.94 relative to its quantized-only baseline (Table 5).
Ablation 'W/O Reranking' yields an average accuracy ratio around 0.88 for Llama-3.1-8B, demonstrating reranking's importance.
Ablation 'W/O Unstable Heads' yields an average accuracy ratio around 0.96 for Llama-3.1-8B, demonstrating the value of treating unstable heads specially.
End-to-end offline throughput experiments (Section 4.4) have been reproduced.
The offline workload consists of 500 prompts randomly sampled from L-Eval, with input lengths between 10k and 30k tokens.
Token throughput is measured at output lengths in {100, 250, 500, 750, 1000, 1250, 1500} for both Llama-3.1-8B and Mistral-7B with FlexiCache-1024, FlexiCache-2048, and vLLM.
FlexiCache improves average token throughput by 1.38–1.55× over vLLM on Llama-3.1-8B across output lengths.
FlexiCache improves average token throughput by 1.44–1.46× over vLLM on Mistral-7B across output lengths.
At output length 500, FlexiCache-1024 achieves up to 1.46× and FlexiCache-2048 up to 1.41× request throughput speedup over vLLM (Figure 5c).
Mistral-Small-24B-Instruct-2501 achieves approximately 1.37× token throughput over vLLM at output length 500 (Figure 7).
Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct achieves approximately 1.33× token throughput over vLLM at output length 500 (Figure 7).
End-to-end online serving experiments (Section 4.4) have been reproduced.

The online workload consists of 500 L-Eval requests with input lengths between 10k–30k and output lengths between 20–2000 tokens, arriving via a Poisson process at request rates {0.20, 0.25, 0.30, 0.35, 0.40} req/s.
Mean TPOT, mean TTFT, and TTFT CDF are measured for vLLM, FlexiCache-1024, and FlexiCache-2048 across request rates.
At 0.4 req/s, FlexiCache-1024 achieves mean TPOT around 34.6 ms vs ≈71.5 ms for vLLM (≈2.1× lower) and FlexiCache-2048 reaches ≈44.9 ms (≈1.6× lower).
TTFT for vLLM collapses (spikes sharply) at request rates above 0.35 req/s, while FlexiCache maintains stable TTFT under the same load (Figure 6b).
At 0.35 req/s, the TTFT CDF for FlexiCache shows a shorter tail than vLLM (Figure 6c).
Microbenchmark experiments (Section 4.5) have been reproduced.
Layer-wise decode latency is measured for dense decode versus FlexiCache sparse decode (1024 and 2048 budgets) at batch sizes {5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40} with 10k prompt tokens per input.
FlexiCache-1024 achieves up to ~4× decode kernel speedup over dense decode at batch size 40 (Figure 8).
Stability-aware reranking is compared against naive reranking (every head reranked every step) across batch sizes 5–40.
Stability-aware reranking is approximately 2.44× faster than naive reranking at batch size 40 (Figure 9).
Rerank-interval × unstable-head-fraction sweep is performed across rerank intervals {16, 32} and unstable fractions {0.0625, 0.125, 0.25}, computing average L-Eval accuracy retention (Table 6).
With 25% unstable heads and rerank interval 16, average L-Eval accuracy retention is approximately 0.99 (Table 6).
GPU memory usage is measured per request across sequence lengths {5k, 10k, 15k, 20k, 25k, 30k} for FlexiCache-1024 and FlexiCache-2048, normalized as a fraction of the dense KV cache.
FlexiCache reduces GPU memory by approximately 70% at sequence lengths ≥20k tokens with a 1024-token budget (Figure 10), approaching the 75% asymptote.

Promoted KV transfer size statistics are reported per task (Table 4), with promoted KV ranging ~44–67 MB (~23–35% of the 192 MB top-K stable-head KV) for Llama-3.1-8B with 192 stable heads and a 2048-token budget.
